1:
"Now Joshua was old and stricken in years; and the LORD said unto him, Thou art old and stricken in years, and there remaineth yet very much land to be possessed." The witnesses note Joshua grown old, with much land yet remaining to be possessed. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua was probably about a hundred, living to a hundred and ten; the land was seven years in dividing. The witnesses note that very much land yet remained to be possessed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Joshua was old — his warring near its end; the the LORD said — thou art old; the much land remaineth — the conquest unfinished; the the promise outruns one life — i follow after, not yet attained (Phil. 3:12).

The witnesses agree the chapter opens with a sober reminder amid the victories — much was won, but "there remaineth yet very much land to be possessed": Joshua was now old, his warring days near their end, yet the conquest was not complete; the unfinished work, set against the aged leader, marks how the fullness of the promise outruns one life's labour; it teaches that the people of God in this life never possess their whole inheritance, always more remaining to be taken, and that the believer, however far advanced, has not yet attained, "not as though I had already attained... but I follow after" (Phil. 3:12), much land still to be possessed.

So the witnesses make the verse much land remaineth: The witnesses note the Joshua was old: his warring near its end. The witnesses note the the LORD said: thou art old. The witnesses note the much land remaineth: the conquest unfinished. The witnesses note the the promise outruns one life: I follow after, not yet attained (Phil. 3:12). The expositors observe the Joshua was old: his warring near its end. The expositors observe the the LORD said: thou art old. The expositors observe the much land remaineth: the conquest unfinished. The expositors observe the the promise outruns one life: I follow after, not yet attained (Phil. 3:12). The believer is taught to mark the Joshua was old, the the LORD said, the much land remaineth, the the promise outruns one life, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that Joshua was old, with much land yet to be possessed — the fullness of the promise outrunning one life's labour, the believer never possessing his whole inheritance here, following after though not yet attained (Phil. 3:12).
2:
"This is the land that yet remaineth: all the borders of the Philistines, and all Geshuri," The witnesses note the LORD naming the land yet unconquered, beginning with the Philistine borders. The witnesses note that Gill notes this account was given for Joshua's information, that he might know what to divide, and for Israel's, that they might know their right. The witnesses note that it begins with the borders of the Philistines. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the this is the land — that yet remaineth; the the Philistine borders — named by God; the for Joshua and Israel — their right assured; the the promise covers all — faithful is he that promised (Heb. 10:23).

The witnesses agree God himself set out the bounds of the land yet to be taken, that nothing of the promise be forgotten: "this is the land that yet remaineth"; Gill notes it was named both for Joshua's guidance in dividing and for the people's assurance of their right; the careful specifying of the unconquered parts shows that God reckons the whole promised inheritance theirs, even what is not yet possessed; it teaches that the promises of God cover all he has pledged, the unclaimed portion still secured by his word, the title sure though the possession be future, "the LORD is faithful, who hath promised" (cf. Heb. 10:23), the remaining land marked out as Israel's by right.

So the witnesses make the verse the land that remaineth: The witnesses note the this is the land: that yet remaineth. The witnesses note the the Philistine borders: named by God. The witnesses note the for Joshua and Israel: their right assured. The witnesses note the the promise covers all: faithful is he that promised (Heb. 10:23). The expositors observe the this is the land: that yet remaineth. The expositors observe the the Philistine borders: named by God. The expositors observe the for Joshua and Israel: their right assured. The expositors observe the the promise covers all: faithful is he that promised (Heb. 10:23). The believer is taught to mark the this is the land, the the Philistine borders, the for Joshua and Israel, the the promise covers all, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that God named the land yet unconquered — the whole promised inheritance reckoned Israel's though not yet possessed, the title sure by his word, faithful is he that promised (Heb. 10:23).
3:
"From Sihor, which is before Egypt, even unto the borders of Ekron northward... the five lords of the Philistines;" The witnesses note the bounds of the unconquered land from the river of Egypt to Ekron, the Philistine lordships. The witnesses note that Gill notes Sihor, which Jarchi and Kimchi take for the Nile, named for its black and turbid waters. The witnesses note that it reached to the borders of Ekron, the five Philistine lords. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from Sihor — before Egypt; the unto Ekron — northward; the the five lords — a stubborn people; the the inheritance contested — through tribulation into the kingdom (Acts 14:22).

The witnesses agree the bounds are drawn with care, from "Sihor, which is before Egypt," to Ekron, taking in the Philistine territory; the naming of the five lordships marks a strong and stubborn people yet to be subdued, who indeed long troubled Israel; the specifying of these enemies shows that much warfare lay ahead, the inheritance not to be had without further struggle; it teaches that the possession of God's promises often meets long and hard resistance, the believer's rest secured by promise yet contested in the taking, "we must through much tribulation enter into the kingdom of God" (Acts 14:22), the Philistine land yet to be won.

So the witnesses make the verse the Philistine bounds: The witnesses note the from Sihor: before Egypt. The witnesses note the unto Ekron: northward. The witnesses note the the five lords: a stubborn people. The witnesses note the the inheritance contested: through tribulation into the kingdom (Acts 14:22). The expositors observe the from Sihor: before Egypt. The expositors observe the unto Ekron: northward. The expositors observe the the five lords: a stubborn people. The expositors observe the the inheritance contested: through tribulation into the kingdom (Acts 14:22). The believer is taught to mark the from Sihor, the unto Ekron, the the five lords, the the inheritance contested, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that the unconquered land reached from the river of Egypt to Ekron, the Philistine lordships — a stubborn people yet to be subdued, the inheritance secured by promise yet contested, through much tribulation entering the kingdom (Acts 14:22).
4:
"From the south, all the land of the Canaanites, and Mearah that is beside the Sidonians, unto Aphek, to the borders of the Amorites:" The witnesses note the further bounds of the land remaining — the Canaanites southward to the Amorite borders. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Canaanites particularly so called, distinct from the other nations, dwelling in several parts. The witnesses note that the land reached to the borders of the Amorites. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from the south — the Canaanites' land; the beside the Sidonians — unto Aphek; the the Amorite borders — the extent remaining; the much still to take — reaching forth to what is before (Phil. 3:13).

The witnesses agree the survey of the unconquered land continues, naming the Canaanite and Amorite territories yet to be taken: "all the land of the Canaanites... unto the borders of the Amorites"; the breadth of what remained shows how much of the promise was still unrealized, even after great victories; the naming of nation after nation marks the extent of the work yet before Israel; it teaches that the people of God, even after notable successes, must reckon how much remains, not resting in past gains while the promise is unfulfilled, "forgetting those things which are behind, and reaching forth unto those... before" (Phil. 3:13), the Canaanite land still to be possessed.

So the witnesses make the verse the Canaanite bounds: The witnesses note the from the south: the Canaanites' land. The witnesses note the beside the Sidonians: unto Aphek. The witnesses note the the Amorite borders: the extent remaining. The witnesses note the much still to take: reaching forth to what is before (Phil. 3:13). The expositors observe the from the south: the Canaanites' land. The expositors observe the beside the Sidonians: unto Aphek. The expositors observe the the Amorite borders: the extent remaining. The expositors observe the much still to take: reaching forth to what is before (Phil. 3:13). The believer is taught to mark the from the south, the beside the Sidonians, the the Amorite borders, the much still to take, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that the land remaining reached from the Canaanites to the Amorite borders — how much of the promise was still unrealized, the people not resting in past gains, reaching forth to what is before (Phil. 3:13).
5:
"And the land of the Giblites, and all Lebanon, toward the sunrising, from Baalgad under mount Hermon unto the entering into Hamath." The witnesses note the land of the Giblites and Lebanon among the territories yet remaining. The witnesses note that Gill notes the land of the Giblites, called Byblus by the Greeks, pleasantly situated by the seaside. The witnesses note that it reached from Baal-gad to the entering of Hamath. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Giblites — and Lebanon; the toward the sunrising — from Baal-gad; the unto Hamath — the far bounds; the the largeness of the gift — above all we ask or think (Eph. 3:20).

The witnesses agree the unconquered land stretched far north, taking in "all Lebanon... unto the entering into Hamath": the inheritance promised reached to these distant bounds, far beyond what was yet held; the great extent of the promise, much of it still unpossessed, magnifies the largeness of God's gift to his people; it teaches that the LORD's promises are wide and full, the inheritance reaching further than present attainment, the believer's portion in Christ exceeding all he has yet entered upon, "able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think" (Eph. 3:20), the land reaching to Hamath.

So the witnesses make the verse unto Hamath: The witnesses note the the Giblites: and Lebanon. The witnesses note the toward the sunrising: from Baal-gad. The witnesses note the unto Hamath: the far bounds. The witnesses note the the largeness of the gift: above all we ask or think (Eph. 3:20). The expositors observe the the Giblites: and Lebanon. The expositors observe the toward the sunrising: from Baal-gad. The expositors observe the unto Hamath: the far bounds. The expositors observe the the largeness of the gift: above all we ask or think (Eph. 3:20). The believer is taught to mark the the Giblites, the toward the sunrising, the unto Hamath, the the largeness of the gift, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that the land remaining reached to Lebanon and the entering of Hamath — the largeness of God's promised gift, the inheritance far exceeding present attainment, above all we ask or think (Eph. 3:20).
6:
"All the inhabitants of the hill country from Lebanon... them will I drive out from before the children of Israel: only divide thou it by lot unto the Israelites for an inheritance, as I have commanded thee." The witnesses note God's promise to drive out the remaining nations, and his command to divide the land by lot. The witnesses note that Gill notes the inhabitants of the hill country in and about Lebanon. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the LORD promising to drive out the Sidonians and all, only divide it by lot. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the hill country — from Lebanon; the them will I drive out — god's own promise; the divide it by lot — by faith; the act on the promise — he will not fail thee (Deut. 31:6).

The witnesses agree God joined his own promise to drive out the enemy with the charge to Joshua to divide the land in faith: "them will I drive out... only divide thou it by lot"; though much remained unconquered, Israel was to apportion it as already given, resting on God's pledge to expel the nations; the dividing by lot before the possession shows the union of faith in the promise with present obedience; it teaches that the people of God act upon his promises as sure, taking possession by faith of what he has pledged to give, "the LORD thy God, he it is that doth go before thee; he will not fail thee" (Deut. 31:6), the land divided in faith on his word to drive out the foe.

So the witnesses make the verse them will I drive out: The witnesses note the the hill country: from Lebanon. The witnesses note the them will I drive out: God's own promise. The witnesses note the divide it by lot: by faith. The witnesses note the act on the promise: he will not fail thee (Deut. 31:6). The expositors observe the the hill country: from Lebanon. The expositors observe the them will I drive out: God's own promise. The expositors observe the divide it by lot: by faith. The expositors observe the act on the promise: he will not fail thee (Deut. 31:6). The believer is taught to mark the the hill country, the them will I drive out, the divide it by lot, the act on the promise, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that God promised to drive out the nations and bade Joshua divide the land by lot — faith in the promise joined with present obedience, the land apportioned as already given, he will not fail thee (Deut. 31:6).
7:
"Now therefore divide this land for an inheritance unto the nine tribes, and the half tribe of Manasseh," The witnesses note the command to divide the land among the nine tribes and half Manasseh. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua, having God's command and authority, was to set about it at once with all diligence. The witnesses note that Henry sets the orders given Joshua to assign each tribe its inheritance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the divide this land — for inheritance; the the nine tribes — and half Manasseh; the by God's command — joshua's charge; the each his portion — a goodly heritage (Ps. 16:6).

The witnesses agree the LORD now charged Joshua to proceed with the dividing of the inheritance among the tribes: "divide this land for an inheritance unto the nine tribes, and the half tribe of Manasseh"; the apportioning of the land to each by God's appointment shows his care that every tribe receive its due portion; the orderly division, Henry notes, was Joshua's charge from God; it teaches that God appoints to each of his people their own inheritance, dividing to every one as he wills, the believer's portion measured out by his hand, "the lines are fallen unto me in pleasant places; yea, I have a goodly heritage" (Ps. 16:6), the land divided to each tribe.

So the witnesses make the verse divide the land: The witnesses note the divide this land: for inheritance. The witnesses note the the nine tribes: and half Manasseh. The witnesses note the by God's command: Joshua's charge. The witnesses note the each his portion: a goodly heritage (Ps. 16:6). The expositors observe the divide this land: for inheritance. The expositors observe the the nine tribes: and half Manasseh. The expositors observe the by God's command: Joshua's charge. The expositors observe the each his portion: a goodly heritage (Ps. 16:6). The believer is taught to mark the divide this land, the the nine tribes, the by God's command, the each his portion, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that Joshua was charged to divide the land among the tribes — God appointing to each his own inheritance, the portion measured out by his hand, the lines fallen in pleasant places (Ps. 16:6).
8:
"With whom the Reubenites and the Gadites have received their inheritance, which Moses gave them, beyond Jordan eastward, even as Moses the servant of the LORD gave them;" The witnesses note that Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh had already received their inheritance east of Jordan from Moses. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was with the other half of Manasseh, those settled beyond Jordan. The witnesses note that Moses the servant of the LORD had given them their inheritance eastward. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Reuben and Gad — already settled; the Moses gave them — beyond Jordan; the the servant of the LORD — divine authority; the each in his place — god not forgetting (Heb. 6:10).

The witnesses agree the verse recalls that two and a half tribes were already settled, having received their portion from Moses east of Jordan: "the Reubenites and the Gadites have received their inheritance, which Moses gave them"; the mention links the present division to the earlier grant, all part of one ordered apportioning under God; the careful noting that "Moses the servant of the LORD" gave it marks the divine authority behind even the eastern settlement; it teaches that God's provision for his people is ordered and complete, each receiving in his appointed time and place, none forgotten, the inheritance dealt out by his servants according to his will, "God is not unrighteous to forget your work" (cf. Heb. 6:10), the eastern tribes already in their portion.

So the witnesses make the verse the eastern tribes: The witnesses note the Reuben and Gad: already settled. The witnesses note the Moses gave them: beyond Jordan. The witnesses note the the servant of the LORD: divine authority. The witnesses note the each in his place: God not forgetting (Heb. 6:10). The expositors observe the Reuben and Gad: already settled. The expositors observe the Moses gave them: beyond Jordan. The expositors observe the the servant of the LORD: divine authority. The expositors observe the each in his place: God not forgetting (Heb. 6:10). The believer is taught to mark the Reuben and Gad, the Moses gave them, the the servant of the LORD, the each in his place, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh had received their inheritance from Moses east of Jordan — God's ordered provision for his people, each receiving in his appointed place, none forgotten (Heb. 6:10).
9:
"From Aroer, that is upon the bank of the river Arnon, and the city that is in the midst of the river, and all the plain of Medeba unto Dibon;" The witnesses note the bounds of the eastern inheritance, beginning from Aroer on the Arnon. The witnesses note that Gill notes Aroer, a city of Moab, the river Arnon being the border between Moab and the Amorites. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the description from Aroer and the plain of Medeba. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from Aroer — on the Arnon; the the city in the river — the precise bound; the the plain of Medeba — unto Dibon; the definite gifts — thou maintainest my lot (Ps. 16:5).

The witnesses agree the description of the eastern territory begins precisely, "from Aroer... upon the bank of the river Arnon... unto Dibon": the careful bounding of the land shows the exactness with which God assigned each portion; nothing was left vague, every border marked; the precision teaches that God's gifts to his people are definite and sure, measured out with care, no part of the inheritance uncertain; it teaches that the LORD knows and appoints the very bounds of his people's portion, "thou maintainest my lot" (Ps. 16:5), the eastern land marked from Aroer to Dibon.

So the witnesses make the verse from Aroer: The witnesses note the from Aroer: on the Arnon. The witnesses note the the city in the river: the precise bound. The witnesses note the the plain of Medeba: unto Dibon. The witnesses note the definite gifts: thou maintainest my lot (Ps. 16:5). The expositors observe the from Aroer: on the Arnon. The expositors observe the the city in the river: the precise bound. The expositors observe the the plain of Medeba: unto Dibon. The expositors observe the definite gifts: thou maintainest my lot (Ps. 16:5). The believer is taught to mark the from Aroer, the the city in the river, the the plain of Medeba, the definite gifts, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that the eastern inheritance was bounded from Aroer on the Arnon — the exactness with which God assigns each portion, his gifts definite and sure, he maintains my lot (Ps. 16:5).
10:
"And all the cities of Sihon king of the Amorites, which reigned in Heshbon, unto the border of the children of Ammon;" The witnesses note the cities of Sihon, the Amorite king, included in the eastern inheritance. The witnesses note that Gill notes Heshbon, a city Sihon took from the Moabites and made his royal seat. The witnesses note that the territory reached to the border of Ammon. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the cities of Sihon — the Amorite king; the reigned in Heshbon — his royal seat; the unto Ammon's border — the territory; the the wicked's land given — the meek inherit (Ps. 37:11).

The witnesses agree the inheritance included all the realm of Sihon, the Amorite king whom Israel had overthrown: "all the cities of Sihon... which reigned in Heshbon"; the land of a conquered king now became the settled possession of God's people, the spoil of victory turned to inheritance; the granting of Sihon's cities shows how God gives his people to dwell in what their enemies once held; it teaches that the LORD bestows on his own the possessions of the wicked, the meek inheriting the earth, "they shall... delight themselves in the abundance of peace" (Ps. 37:11), Sihon's cities now Israel's heritage.

So the witnesses make the verse the cities of Sihon: The witnesses note the the cities of Sihon: the Amorite king. The witnesses note the reigned in Heshbon: his royal seat. The witnesses note the unto Ammon's border: the territory. The witnesses note the the wicked's land given: the meek inherit (Ps. 37:11). The expositors observe the the cities of Sihon: the Amorite king. The expositors observe the reigned in Heshbon: his royal seat. The expositors observe the unto Ammon's border: the territory. The expositors observe the the wicked's land given: the meek inherit (Ps. 37:11). The believer is taught to mark the the cities of Sihon, the reigned in Heshbon, the unto Ammon's border, the the wicked's land given, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that the inheritance included Sihon's cities — the realm of a conquered king turned to Israel's possession, God giving his people the lands the wicked once held, the meek inheriting the earth (Ps. 37:11).
11:
"And Gilead, and the border of the Geshurites and Maachathites, and all mount Hermon, and all Bashan unto Salcah;" The witnesses note Gilead, the Geshurite and Maachathite borders, Hermon, and Bashan in the inheritance. The witnesses note that Gill notes Gilead was part of the kingdom of Og, half given to Reuben and half to Gad. The witnesses note that Clarke and Wesley set the borders of the Geshurites and Maachathites. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Gilead — part of Og's realm; the the Geshurite borders — and Maachathite; the Hermon and Bashan — the fertile land; the a rich inheritance — richly all things to enjoy (1 Tim. 6:17).

The witnesses agree the eastern inheritance took in the rich lands of Gilead and Bashan, with Hermon's heights: "Gilead... all mount Hermon, and all Bashan unto Salcah"; the famed and fertile region, once the kingdom of Og, was now divided among the tribes; the granting of so goodly a land shows the bounty of God's provision for his people; yet, as the next verses note, some borders remained unsubdued; it teaches that God gives his people a rich inheritance, abundant and good, the portion of his own a generous heritage, "he giveth us richly all things to enjoy" (1 Tim. 6:17), Gilead and Bashan made Israel's.

So the witnesses make the verse Gilead and Bashan: The witnesses note the Gilead: part of Og's realm. The witnesses note the the Geshurite borders: and Maachathite. The witnesses note the Hermon and Bashan: the fertile land. The witnesses note the a rich inheritance: richly all things to enjoy (1 Tim. 6:17). The expositors observe the Gilead: part of Og's realm. The expositors observe the the Geshurite borders: and Maachathite. The expositors observe the Hermon and Bashan: the fertile land. The expositors observe the a rich inheritance: richly all things to enjoy (1 Tim. 6:17). The believer is taught to mark the Gilead, the the Geshurite borders, the Hermon and Bashan, the a rich inheritance, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that the inheritance took in Gilead, Hermon, and Bashan — the bounty of God's provision, a rich and goodly heritage given his people, who gives us richly all things to enjoy (1 Tim. 6:17).
12:
"All the kingdom of Og in Bashan, which reigned in Ashtaroth and in Edrei, who remained of the remnant of the giants: for these did Moses smite, and cast them out." The witnesses note the kingdom of Og, last of the giants, smitten by Moses and given for inheritance. The witnesses note that Gill refers to Og reigning in Ashtaroth and Edrei. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Og as remaining of the remnant of the giants, whom Moses smote and cast out. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all the kingdom of Og — in Bashan; the the remnant of the giants — the last of them; the Moses smote them — and cast them out; the no foe too mighty — the LORD mightier than many waters (Ps. 93:4).

The witnesses agree the inheritance embraced the realm of Og, "who remained of the remnant of the giants," overthrown by Moses: the kingdom of the last and mightiest of the giants now belonged to God's people; the felling of so great a foe and the granting of his land magnify the power of God who gives even the strongholds of giants to the meek; it teaches that no enemy is too mighty for the LORD to subdue, the greatest of his foes cast out and their possessions given to his own, "the LORD on high is mightier than the noise of many waters" (Ps. 93:4), the giant Og's kingdom now Israel's heritage.

So the witnesses make the verse the kingdom of Og: The witnesses note the all the kingdom of Og: in Bashan. The witnesses note the the remnant of the giants: the last of them. The witnesses note the Moses smote them: and cast them out. The witnesses note the no foe too mighty: the LORD mightier than many waters (Ps. 93:4). The expositors observe the all the kingdom of Og: in Bashan. The expositors observe the the remnant of the giants: the last of them. The expositors observe the Moses smote them: and cast them out. The expositors observe the no foe too mighty: the LORD mightier than many waters (Ps. 93:4). The believer is taught to mark the all the kingdom of Og, the the remnant of the giants, the Moses smote them, the no foe too mighty, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that the inheritance embraced Og's kingdom, last of the giants whom Moses smote — no enemy too mighty for the LORD, the strongholds of giants given to the meek, the LORD mightier than the many waters (Ps. 93:4).
13:
"Nevertheless the children of Israel expelled not the Geshurites, nor the Maachathites: but the Geshurites and the Maachathites dwell among the Israelites until this day." The witnesses note Israel's failure to expel the Geshurites and Maachathites, who remained among them. The witnesses note that Gill notes they expelled them neither in the times of Moses nor of Joshua. The witnesses note that they dwelt among the Israelites until this day. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the expelled not — the Geshurites; the nor the Maachathites — left in the land; the dwell among them — to this day; the partial obedience a snare — mingled, and learned their works (Ps. 106:34-35).

The witnesses agree the verse marks a blemish on the conquest — the incomplete obedience that left enemies in the land: "the children of Israel expelled not the Geshurites... but [they] dwell among the Israelites"; what God had bidden them drive out, they suffered to remain, a failure that would prove a snare; the leaving of these peoples among them teaches the danger of partial obedience, the unconquered remnant becoming a thorn and temptation; it warns the people of God against sparing what he has charged them to put away, "they did not destroy the nations... but were mingled among the heathen, and learned their works" (Ps. 106:34-35), the Geshurites left to dwell among Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse expelled them not: The witnesses note the expelled not: the Geshurites. The witnesses note the nor the Maachathites: left in the land. The witnesses note the dwell among them: to this day. The witnesses note the partial obedience a snare: mingled, and learned their works (Ps. 106:34-35). The expositors observe the expelled not: the Geshurites. The expositors observe the nor the Maachathites: left in the land. The expositors observe the dwell among them: to this day. The expositors observe the partial obedience a snare: mingled, and learned their works (Ps. 106:34-35). The believer is taught to mark the expelled not, the nor the Maachathites, the dwell among them, the partial obedience a snare, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that Israel expelled not the Geshurites and Maachathites, who remained among them — the danger of partial obedience, the unconquered remnant a snare, the nations mingled and their works learned (Ps. 106:34-35).
14:
"Only unto the tribe of Levi he gave none inheritance; the sacrifices of the LORD God of Israel made by fire are their inheritance, as he said unto them." The witnesses note that Levi received no land, for the offerings of the LORD are their inheritance. The witnesses note that Gill notes neither the Lord nor Moses appointed Levi any land inheritance, for the Lord was to be their inheritance. The witnesses note that Calvin reflects on Levi's portion in the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Levi none inheritance — no land; the the sacrifices of the LORD — their portion; the to live by the altar — a higher heritage; the God their portion — the LORD the portion of mine inheritance (Ps. 16:5).

The witnesses agree Levi's portion was of a higher kind — not land, but the LORD himself and his offerings: "unto the tribe of Levi he gave none inheritance; the sacrifices of the LORD... are their inheritance"; the priestly tribe was to live by the altar and to have God for their portion, a richer heritage than fields; Gill marks that "the Lord was to be their inheritance"; it teaches that those who serve God find their portion in him, the minister's true inheritance not earthly but divine, a figure of every believer whose treasure is the LORD himself, "the LORD is the portion of mine inheritance... I have a goodly heritage" (Ps. 16:5), Levi's inheritance the offerings and God himself.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD their inheritance: The witnesses note the Levi none inheritance: no land. The witnesses note the the sacrifices of the LORD: their portion. The witnesses note the to live by the altar: a higher heritage. The witnesses note the God their portion: the LORD the portion of mine inheritance (Ps. 16:5). The expositors observe the Levi none inheritance: no land. The expositors observe the the sacrifices of the LORD: their portion. The expositors observe the to live by the altar: a higher heritage. The expositors observe the God their portion: the LORD the portion of mine inheritance (Ps. 16:5). The believer is taught to mark the Levi none inheritance, the the sacrifices of the LORD, the to live by the altar, the God their portion, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that Levi received no land, the offerings of the LORD their inheritance — those who serve God finding their portion in him, the minister's true inheritance divine, the LORD the portion of mine inheritance (Ps. 16:5).
15:
"And Moses gave unto the tribe of the children of Reuben inheritance according to their families." The witnesses note Moses giving Reuben its inheritance according to its families. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was given according to the number of them, and sufficient for them. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Moses giving Reuben its inheritance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses gave — to Reuben; the according to their families — measured to fit; the sufficient for them — as was meet; the apportioned fairly — to every man as he had need (Acts 4:35).

The witnesses agree the dividing now turns to detail the portion of each eastern tribe, beginning with Reuben: "Moses gave unto... Reuben inheritance according to their families"; the apportioning "according to their families" shows that the land was measured to fit the number, each tribe receiving as was meet; the care to suit the portion to the people marks God's fairness in dividing his gifts; it teaches that the LORD apportions to his people as is fitting for each, none over-burdened or under-served, "distribution was made unto every man according as he had need" (Acts 4:35), Reuben's inheritance measured to its families.

So the witnesses make the verse Reuben's inheritance: The witnesses note the Moses gave: to Reuben. The witnesses note the according to their families: measured to fit. The witnesses note the sufficient for them: as was meet. The witnesses note the apportioned fairly: to every man as he had need (Acts 4:35). The expositors observe the Moses gave: to Reuben. 16:
"And their coast was from Aroer, that is on the bank of the river Arnon, and the city that is in the midst of the river, and all the plain by Medeba;" The witnesses note the bounds of Reuben's coast, from Aroer and the Arnon. The witnesses note that Gill notes the country of Sihon is so described, showing it was his land given to Reuben, though not all of it. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the coast from Aroer and the plain by Medeba. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from Aroer — on the Arnon; the the city in the river — the bound; the the plain by Medeba — reuben's portion; the a definite heritage — the bounds of their habitation (Acts 17:26).

The witnesses agree Reuben's portion is bounded with the same care as the whole, "from Aroer... and all the plain by Medeba": the precise marking of the tribe's coast shows again the exactness of God's dividing, each border fixed; the land that had been Sihon's now became Reuben's settled inheritance; the careful bounding teaches that God assigns to each of his people a definite portion, the believer's lot marked out by his hand and made secure; it teaches that the LORD gives a sure and bounded heritage, "the lot of their inheritance" fixed by his appointment, "he hath made... the bounds of their habitation" (cf. Acts 17:26), Reuben's coast marked from Aroer.

So the witnesses make the verse Reuben's coast: The witnesses note the from Aroer: on the Arnon. The witnesses note the the city in the river: the bound. 17:
"Heshbon, and all her cities that are in the plain; Dibon, and Bamothbaal, and Bethbaalmeon," The witnesses note the cities of Reuben's portion — Heshbon, Dibon, and the rest in the plain. The witnesses note that Gill notes Heshbon and her cities in the plain, by Medeba, reaching to Dibon. The witnesses note that Clarke notes Bamoth-baal, the high places of Baal, from altars on hills for idolatrous worship. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Heshbon — and her cities; the Dibon — in the plain; the Bamoth-baal — the high places of Baal; the the polluted cleansed — come out and be separate (2 Cor. 6:15-17).

The witnesses agree the naming of Reuben's cities includes places once given to idolatry — "Bamothbaal" and "Bethbaalmeon," the high places of Baal: Clarke marks these names as relics of the Canaanite worship that had defiled the land; the very towns that had been seats of false worship now passed to God's people, to be cleansed and possessed; it teaches that God gives his people to inherit what sin had polluted, that they might purge it and devote it to him, the places of idols to become the dwellings of the LORD's own, "what concord hath Christ with Belial?... come out from among them, and be ye separate" (2 Cor. 6:15-17), the high places of Baal now Reuben's to possess.

So the witnesses make the verse the cities of Reuben: The witnesses note the Heshbon: and her cities. The witnesses note the Dibon: in the plain. The witnesses note the Bamoth-baal: the high places of Baal. The witnesses note the the polluted cleansed: come out and be separate (2 Cor. 6:15-17). 18:
"And Jahazah, and Kedemoth, and Mephaath," The witnesses note further cities of Reuben's inheritance, some afterward given to the Levites. The witnesses note that Gill notes Jahazah, called Jahaz, where the battle was fought between Sihon and Israel. The witnesses note that Clarke notes Jahaza was given to the Levites (1 Chr. 6:78). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Jahazah — the field of battle; the Kedemoth — and Mephaath; the given to the Levites — a holy use; the war turned to peace — every man under his vine (Mic. 4:4).

The witnesses agree the naming of Reuben's cities includes Jahaz, the very field where Sihon was overthrown, now a settled town of Israel: Clarke notes it was afterward given to the Levites; the place of battle became a place of peaceable dwelling, and a seat of those who served the LORD; the listing of these towns shows the orderly settlement of the land once won by war; it teaches that what God gives his people through conflict is turned to quiet possession and holy use, the field of victory become a dwelling and a Levite city, "they shall sit every man under his vine and under his fig tree" (Mic. 4:4), Jahaz now Reuben's and the Levites'.

So the witnesses make the verse Jahaz and the cities: The witnesses note the Jahazah: the field of battle. The witnesses note the Kedemoth: and Mephaath. The witnesses note the given to the Levites: a holy use. The witnesses note the war turned to peace: every man under his vine (Mic. 4:4). The expositors observe the Jahazah: the field of battle. The expositors observe the Kedemoth: and Mephaath. The expositors observe the given to the Levites: a holy use. The expositors observe the war turned to peace: every man under his vine (Mic. 4:4). The verse thus records that Reuben's cities included Jahaz, the field of Sihon's defeat, later a Levite town — what God gives through conflict turned to quiet possession and holy use, every man under his vine (Mic. 4:4).
19:
"And Kirjathaim, and Sibmah, and Zarethshahar in the mount of the valley," The witnesses note more of Reuben's cities, lands that had passed through many hands before becoming Israel's. The witnesses note that Gill notes Kirjathaim, of which he speaks elsewhere. The witnesses note that Clarke notes it passed from the Emim to the Moabites, to the Amorites, and so to Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Kirjathaim — and Sibmah; the many possessors — emim to Amorites; the at last to Israel — god's purpose; the kingdoms rise and fall — he removeth and setteth up kings (Dan. 2:21).

The witnesses agree the history of these towns, Clarke notes, was one of many changes — "from the Emim to the Moabites... to the Amorites" and at last to Israel: the land had passed through possessor after possessor before coming to God's people; the changing of hands shows how the kingdoms of men rise and fall, while God's purpose carries the inheritance at last to his own; it teaches that the LORD overrules the shifting fortunes of the nations to bring his people into their promised portion, the much-contested land settling at last where he appointed, "he removeth kings, and setteth up kings" (Dan. 2:21), Kirjathaim come at last to Reuben.

So the witnesses make the verse the much-changed land: The witnesses note the Kirjathaim: and Sibmah. The witnesses note the many possessors: Emim to Amorites. The witnesses note the at last to Israel: God's purpose. The witnesses note the kingdoms rise and fall: he removeth and setteth up kings (Dan. 2:21). 20:
"And Bethpeor, and Ashdothpisgah, and Bethjeshimoth," The witnesses note Beth-peor among Reuben's cities, named from the idol Peor. The witnesses note that Gill notes Beth-peor, so called from Peor, the idol of the Moabites, likely having a temple to its honour; over against it lay the valley where Israel abode. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Beth-peor among the cities. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Beth-peor — named from the idol; the a seat of idolatry — the place of Israel's sin; the now Reuben's — the ground possessed; the grace over sin — where sin abounded, grace abounded (Rom. 5:20).

The witnesses agree this city, "Beth-peor," bore the name of the Moabite idol and had been a seat of that shameful worship which once ensnared Israel (Num. 25:3): the place of the people's grievous sin in the matter of Baal-peor now passed into their own possession; the granting of the very ground of their former fall shows God's grace in turning the place of sin into a place of inheritance; it teaches that the LORD can redeem even the scenes of his people's shame, the seat of idolatry becoming their own to cleanse and hold, "where sin abounded, grace did much more abound" (Rom. 5:20), Beth-peor now Reuben's portion.

So the witnesses make the verse Beth-peor: The witnesses note the Beth-peor: named from the idol. The witnesses note the a seat of idolatry: the place of Israel's sin. The witnesses note the now Reuben's: the ground possessed. The witnesses note the grace over sin: where sin abounded, grace abounded (Rom. 5:20). 21:
"And all the cities of the plain, and all the kingdom of Sihon king of the Amorites, which reigned in Heshbon, whom Moses smote with the princes of Midian..." The witnesses note all the kingdom of Sihon, smitten by Moses together with the princes of Midian. The witnesses note that Gill notes the cities of the plain in the champaign country. The witnesses note that Clarke refers to the war with Midian (Num. 31), whence this verse seems drawn. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the cities of the plain — the champaign; the all Sihon's kingdom — the realm taken; the the princes of Midian — smitten with him; the the enemies fallen — so let all thine enemies perish (Judg. 5:31).

The witnesses agree the inheritance comprised "all the kingdom of Sihon," whom Moses overthrew along with the princes of Midian: the whole realm of a defeated king, and the recollection of the Midianite war, are joined to mark the completeness of the conquest east of Jordan; the naming of the princes slain recalls God's judgment on those who had sought Israel's ruin; it teaches that the LORD gives his people the lands of their conquered enemies, and that those who war against his people are themselves brought down, "all thine enemies... shall perish" (Judg. 5:31), Sihon's kingdom and the Midianite princes alike fallen.

So the witnesses make the verse the kingdom of Sihon: The witnesses note the the cities of the plain: the champaign. The witnesses note the all Sihon's kingdom: the realm taken. The witnesses note the the princes of Midian: smitten with him. The witnesses note the the enemies fallen: so let all thine enemies perish (Judg. 5:31). The verse thus records that the inheritance comprised Sihon's kingdom and recalled the Midianite princes Moses smote — the LORD giving his people the lands of conquered foes, those who war against his people brought down, so let all thine enemies perish (Judg. 5:31).
22:
"Balaam also the son of Beor, the soothsayer, did the children of Israel slay with the sword among them that were slain by them." The witnesses note the death of Balaam the soothsayer, slain with the sword among the Midianites. The witnesses note that Gill notes Balaam was slain at the same time as the princes of Midian; Kimchi supposes he had returned to Midian for his wages of counsel against Israel. The witnesses note that he was slain with the sword. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Balaam the soothsayer — the false prophet; the slain with the sword — his violent end; the among the Midianites — whom he served for gain; the the doom of covetous prophecy — who loved the wages of unrighteousness (2 Pet. 2:15).

The witnesses agree the fall of Balaam is recorded as a solemn judgment on the false prophet who sold his gifts against God's people: "Balaam... the soothsayer, did the children of Israel slay with the sword"; he who could not curse Israel taught Balak to seduce them, and came at last to a violent end among the very people he had served for gain; his death is the doom of those who, knowing God, oppose his people for reward; it teaches the wretched end of covetous false prophecy, "the way of Balaam... who loved the wages of unrighteousness" (2 Pet. 2:15), and "perished in the gainsaying of Core" (Jude 11), Balaam slain among the Midianites.

So the witnesses make the verse Balaam slain: The witnesses note the Balaam the soothsayer: the false prophet. The witnesses note the slain with the sword: his violent end. The witnesses note the among the Midianites: whom he served for gain. The witnesses note the the doom of covetous prophecy: who loved the wages of unrighteousness (2 Pet. 2:15). The expositors observe the among the Midianites: whom he served for gain. The expositors observe the the doom of covetous prophecy: who loved the wages of unrighteousness (2 Pet. 2:15). The believer is taught to mark the Balaam the soothsayer, the slain with the sword, the among the Midianites, the the doom of covetous prophecy, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that Balaam the soothsayer was slain with the sword — the solemn doom of the false prophet who sold his gifts against God's people, the way of Balaam who loved the wages of unrighteousness (2 Pet. 2:15; Jude 11).
23:
"And the border of the children of Reuben was Jordan, and the border thereof. This was the inheritance of the children of Reuben after their families, the cities and the villages thereof." The witnesses note Jordan as the western border of Reuben, completing the description of its inheritance. The witnesses note that Gill notes their eastern border was Aroer on the Arnon, their western the river Jordan. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the cities and villages, the villages being unwalled hamlets. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the border was Jordan — the western bound; the the inheritance of Reuben — complete; the the cities and villages — its towns; the a settled portion — the disposing is of the LORD (Prov. 16:33).

The witnesses agree the account of Reuben's portion closes with its full bounds and its towns: "the border... was Jordan... This was the inheritance of the children of Reuben"; from the Arnon to the Jordan, with cities and villages, the tribe's heritage is set down complete; the rounded description marks the certainty and fullness of the portion God assigned; it teaches that the LORD gives his people a settled and entire inheritance, bounded and sure, the believer's lot complete in his appointment, "the lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD" (Prov. 16:33), Reuben's inheritance fully described.

So the witnesses make the verse Reuben's inheritance: The witnesses note the the border was Jordan: the western bound. The witnesses note the the inheritance of Reuben: complete. The witnesses note the the cities and villages: its towns. The witnesses note the a settled portion: the disposing is of the LORD (Prov. 16:33). The expositors observe the the border was Jordan: the western bound. The expositors observe the the inheritance of Reuben: complete. The expositors observe the the cities and villages: its towns. The expositors observe the a settled portion: the disposing is of the LORD (Prov. 16:33). The verse thus records that Jordan bounded Reuben on the west, completing its inheritance — the LORD giving a settled and entire portion, bounded and sure, the whole disposing of the lot his (Prov. 16:33).
24:
"And Moses gave inheritance unto the tribe of Gad, even unto the children of Gad according to their families." The witnesses note Moses giving Gad its inheritance east of Jordan, according to its families. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses gave it on the other side Jordan, as he did to Reuben. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the inheritance of Gad according to their families. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses gave — to Gad; the according to their families — measured to fit; the east of Jordan — like Reuben; the impartial provision — the same care for each member (1 Cor. 12:25).

The witnesses agree the dividing proceeds to Gad, settled like Reuben east of Jordan: "Moses gave inheritance unto the tribe of Gad... according to their families"; each tribe in turn received its appointed portion, measured to its number, the same care shown to all; the orderly progress from tribe to tribe marks God's impartial provision for every part of his people; it teaches that the LORD forgets none of his own, giving to each their due inheritance in its turn, "that there be no schism in the body; but that the members should have the same care one for another" (1 Cor. 12:25), Gad receiving its portion like the rest.

So the witnesses make the verse Gad's inheritance: The witnesses note the Moses gave: to Gad. The witnesses note the according to their families: measured to fit. The witnesses note the east of Jordan: like Reuben. The witnesses note the impartial provision: the same care for each member (1 Cor. 12:25). The expositors observe the Moses gave: to Gad. The expositors observe the according to their families: measured to fit. The expositors observe the east of Jordan: like Reuben. The expositors observe the impartial provision: the same care for each member (1 Cor. 12:25). The believer is taught to mark the Moses gave, the according to their families, the east of Jordan, the impartial provision, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that Moses gave Gad its inheritance according to its families — God's impartial provision for every part of his people, each receiving its due in turn, the same care for each member (1 Cor. 12:25).
25:
"And their coast was Jazer, and all the cities of Gilead, and half the land of the children of Ammon, unto Aroer that is before Rabbah;" The witnesses note Gad's coast — Jazer, the cities of Gilead, and part of the land of Ammon. The witnesses note that Gill notes Jazer, their southern coast, spoken of elsewhere as a city of Moab. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the coast of Gad. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Jazer — the southern coast; the the cities of Gilead — the rich pasture; the half the land of Ammon — to Aroer; the a fitting portion — my God shall supply all your need (Phil. 4:19).

The witnesses agree Gad's portion took in the goodly land of Gilead and reached to the borders of Ammon: "their coast was Jazer, and all the cities of Gilead"; the rich pasture-land of Gilead, suited to the cattle for which Gad and Reuben had asked it (Num. 32:1), became their settled home; the granting of land fit for their substance shows God's care to provide for his people according to their need and calling; it teaches that the LORD gives a portion suited to each, supplying what is fitting for them, "my God shall supply all your need" (Phil. 4:19), Gilead given to the cattle-rich tribe of Gad.

So the witnesses make the verse Gad's coast: The witnesses note the Jazer: the southern coast. The witnesses note the the cities of Gilead: the rich pasture. The witnesses note the half the land of Ammon: to Aroer. The witnesses note the a fitting portion: my God shall supply all your need (Phil. 4:19). The expositors observe the Jazer: the southern coast. The expositors observe the the cities of Gilead: the rich pasture. The expositors observe the half the land of Ammon: to Aroer. The expositors observe the a fitting portion: my God shall supply all your need (Phil. 4:19). The believer is taught to mark the Jazer, the the cities of Gilead, the half the land of Ammon, the a fitting portion, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that Gad's coast took in Jazer and the cities of Gilead — the rich pasture-land suited to their cattle, God providing a portion fitting each according to need, supplying all their need (Phil. 4:19).
26:
"And from Heshbon unto Ramathmizpeh, and Betonim; and from Mahanaim unto the border of Debir;" The witnesses note Gad's eastern and northern bounds, from Heshbon to Ramath-mizpeh and Mahanaim. The witnesses note that Gill notes this describes their border from south to north; Ramath-mizpeh, the same with Ramoth-gilead. The witnesses note that it reached from Mahanaim to the border of Debir. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from Heshbon — to Ramath-mizpeh; the Ramoth-gilead — a city of refuge; the Mahanaim — where Jacob met angels; the ground hallowed by God — this is God's host (Gen. 32:2).

The witnesses agree Gad's bounds are traced through places of note, including Ramath-mizpeh — the later Ramoth-gilead, a city of refuge — and Mahanaim, where Jacob met the angels of God (Gen. 32:2): the land given to Gad held sites rich in the history of God's dealings with his people; the inheritance was not bare ground but ground hallowed by his presence and providence; it teaches that God settles his people in places where his name and works are remembered, the inheritance bound up with his faithfulness of old, "this is God's host" (Gen. 32:2), Gad's land taking in Mahanaim and Ramoth-gilead.

So the witnesses make the verse Gad's bounds: The witnesses note the from Heshbon: to Ramath-mizpeh. The witnesses note the Ramoth-gilead: a city of refuge. The witnesses note the Mahanaim: where Jacob met angels. The witnesses note the ground hallowed by God: this is God's host (Gen. 32:2). The expositors observe the from Heshbon: to Ramath-mizpeh. The expositors observe the Ramoth-gilead: a city of refuge. The expositors observe the Mahanaim: where Jacob met angels. The expositors observe the ground hallowed by God: this is God's host (Gen. 32:2). The believer is taught to mark the from Heshbon, the Ramoth-gilead, the Mahanaim, the ground hallowed by God, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that Gad's bounds took in Ramath-mizpeh and Mahanaim — the inheritance ground hallowed by God's past dealings, settled where his name and works are remembered, this is God's host (Gen. 32:2).
27:
"And in the valley, Betharam, and Bethnimrah, and Succoth, and Zaphon... the rest of the kingdom of Sihon... Jordan and his border, even unto the edge of the sea of Chinnereth on the other side Jordan eastward." The witnesses note the cities of Gad in the valley, bounded by Jordan and the sea of Chinnereth. The witnesses note that Gill notes Beth-aram, the same with Beth-haran. The witnesses note that Clarke notes Beth-aram was rebuilt by Herod and called Livias, after Livia. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the in the valley — beth-aram and the cities; the the rest of Sihon's kingdom — given to Gad; the Jordan and Chinnereth — the bounds; the a fixed portion — the earth abideth for ever (Eccl. 1:4).

The witnesses agree Gad's portion reached into the valley and was bounded by the Jordan and the sea of Chinnereth (Galilee): "in the valley, Betharam... unto the edge of the sea of Chinnereth"; Clarke notes Beth-aram was long after rebuilt and renamed by Herod — a reminder that the cities of God's gift passed through the changes of ages; the bounding by the well-known waters fixes Gad's heritage clearly; it teaches that God gives a definite and well-marked portion, secure within its bounds, the inheritance of his people fixed by his hand even as the generations and their names change, "the earth abideth for ever" (Eccl. 1:4) while men pass, Gad's land bounded by Jordan and the sea.

So the witnesses make the verse unto Chinnereth: The witnesses note the in the valley: Beth-aram and the cities. The witnesses note the the rest of Sihon's kingdom: given to Gad. The witnesses note the Jordan and Chinnereth: the bounds. The witnesses note the a fixed portion: the earth abideth for ever (Eccl. 1:4). The expositors observe the in the valley: Beth-aram and the cities. The expositors observe the the rest of Sihon's kingdom: given to Gad. The expositors observe the Jordan and Chinnereth: the bounds. The verse thus records that Gad's portion reached to the sea of Chinnereth, bounded by Jordan — a definite and well-marked heritage fixed by God's hand though the ages and names change, the earth abiding while men pass (Eccl. 1:4).
28:
"This is the inheritance of the children of Gad after their families, the cities, and their villages." The witnesses note the close of Gad's inheritance — its cities and villages complete. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is the inheritance as described. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the cities and villages of Gad. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the this is the inheritance — of Gad; the after their families — measured to them; the the cities and villages — complete; the a full portion — all things are yours (1 Cor. 3:21-23).

The witnesses agree the account of Gad, like Reuben's, is rounded off with its full complement of towns: "this is the inheritance of the children of Gad... the cities, and their villages"; the complete listing marks the entireness and certainty of the portion God assigned; nothing was left in doubt, the tribe's heritage set down whole; it teaches that the LORD's provision for his people is full and definite, each receiving a complete inheritance, the believer's portion in Christ entire and secure, "all things are yours... and ye are Christ's" (1 Cor. 3:21-23), Gad's inheritance fully set down.

So the witnesses make the verse Gad's inheritance complete: The witnesses note the this is the inheritance: of Gad. The witnesses note the after their families: measured to them. The witnesses note the the cities and villages: complete. The witnesses note the a full portion: all things are yours (1 Cor. 3:21-23). The expositors observe the this is the inheritance: of Gad. The expositors observe the after their families: measured to them. The expositors observe the the cities and villages: complete. The expositors observe the a full portion: all things are yours (1 Cor. 3:21-23). The believer is taught to mark the this is the inheritance, the after their families, the the cities and villages, the a full portion, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that the inheritance of Gad is set down complete with its cities and villages — the LORD's provision full and definite, each receiving an entire portion, all things yours in Christ (1 Cor. 3:21-23).
29:
"And Moses gave inheritance unto the half tribe of Manasseh: and this was the possession of the half tribe of the children of Manasseh by their families." The witnesses note Moses giving the half tribe of Manasseh its inheritance east of Jordan. The witnesses note that Gill notes whether at their request or of himself, there being land enough, is not certain. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the half tribe of Manasseh settled when the tribes were divided. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses gave — to half Manasseh; the the possession — east of Jordan; the by their families — their portion; the varied appointments — diversities of gifts, the same Spirit (1 Cor. 12:4).

The witnesses agree the eastern settlement is completed with the half tribe of Manasseh: "Moses gave inheritance unto the half tribe of Manasseh... by their families"; this portion of Joseph's line was settled beyond Jordan with Reuben and Gad, while the other half received its lot in Canaan; the dividing of one tribe between two regions shows the variety of God's appointments among his people; it teaches that the LORD allots to each as he sees fit, some in one place and some in another, all within his ordering, "there are diversities of gifts... but the same Spirit" (1 Cor. 12:4), half Manasseh settled east of Jordan.

So the witnesses make the verse half Manasseh: The witnesses note the Moses gave: to half Manasseh. The witnesses note the the possession: east of Jordan. The witnesses note the by their families: their portion. The witnesses note the varied appointments: diversities of gifts, the same Spirit (1 Cor. 12:4). The expositors observe the Moses gave: to half Manasseh. The expositors observe the the possession: east of Jordan. The expositors observe the by their families: their portion. The expositors observe the varied appointments: diversities of gifts, the same Spirit (1 Cor. 12:4). The believer is taught to mark the Moses gave, the the possession, the by their families, the varied appointments, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that Moses gave half Manasseh its inheritance east of Jordan — the variety of God's appointments among his people, allotting to each as he sees fit, diversities of gifts by the same Spirit (1 Cor. 12:4).
30:
"And their coast was from Mahanaim, all Bashan, all the kingdom of Og king of Bashan, and all the towns of Jair, which are in Bashan, threescore cities:" The witnesses note the coast of half Manasseh — all Bashan, the kingdom of Og, and the threescore towns of Jair. The witnesses note that Gill notes Mahanaim, a place in the tribe of Gad, was the boundary that way. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set all Bashan and the kingdom of Og given to half Manasseh. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from Mahanaim — the boundary; the all Bashan — og's kingdom; the threescore cities — the towns of Jair; the a liberal gift — an inheritance among the sanctified (Acts 26:18).

The witnesses agree half Manasseh received the great realm of Bashan, with "threescore cities" — the towns of Jair: a wide and rich inheritance, the whole kingdom of the giant Og; the abundance of the portion, sixty cities and all Bashan, shows the largeness of God's gift even to half a tribe; it teaches that the LORD gives liberally to his people, an ample inheritance beyond their deserving, the spoil of giants made their dwelling, "he hath given... an inheritance" (cf. Acts 26:18, an inheritance among them that are sanctified), half Manasseh given all Bashan and the sixty cities.

So the witnesses make the verse all Bashan: The witnesses note the from Mahanaim: the boundary. The witnesses note the all Bashan: Og's kingdom. The witnesses note the threescore cities: the towns of Jair. The witnesses note the a liberal gift: an inheritance among the sanctified (Acts 26:18). The expositors observe the from Mahanaim: the boundary. The expositors observe the all Bashan: Og's kingdom. The expositors observe the threescore cities: the towns of Jair. The expositors observe the a liberal gift: an inheritance among the sanctified (Acts 26:18). The believer is taught to mark the from Mahanaim, the all Bashan, the threescore cities, the a liberal gift, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that half Manasseh received all Bashan and the sixty cities of Jair — the largeness of God's gift, an ample inheritance beyond desert, an inheritance among them that are sanctified (Acts 26:18).
31:
"And half Gilead, and Ashtaroth, and Edrei, cities of the kingdom of Og in Bashan, were pertaining unto the children of Machir the son of Manasseh..." The witnesses note half Gilead and the cities of Og given to the children of Machir, son of Manasseh. The witnesses note that Gill notes the half of Gilead not given to Gad, which Og had possessed, given to Manasseh. The witnesses note that Wesley notes the children of Machir, before called the children of Manasseh. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the half Gilead — not given to Gad; the Ashtaroth and Edrei — the cities of Og; the the children of Machir — valiant men; the reward of labour — thou shalt eat the labour of thine hands (Ps. 128:2).

The witnesses agree the portion of Machir's line included half Gilead and the royal cities of Og, Ashtaroth and Edrei: the descendants of Machir, valiant men who had taken Gilead (Num. 32:39), received the land they had helped to win; the granting to Machir of the cities he had conquered shows God rewarding the labour and valour of his people with possession; it teaches that the LORD gives his people to enjoy the fruit of their faithful service, the inheritance answering to their labour, "thou shalt eat the labour of thine hands: happy shalt thou be" (Ps. 128:2), Machir's sons given the cities of Og.

So the witnesses make the verse Machir's portion: The witnesses note the half Gilead: not given to Gad. The witnesses note the Ashtaroth and Edrei: the cities of Og. The witnesses note the the children of Machir: valiant men. The witnesses note the reward of labour: thou shalt eat the labour of thine hands (Ps. 128:2). The expositors observe the half Gilead: not given to Gad. The expositors observe the Ashtaroth and Edrei: the cities of Og. The expositors observe the the children of Machir: valiant men. The expositors observe the reward of labour: thou shalt eat the labour of thine hands (Ps. 128:2). The believer is taught to mark the half Gilead, the Ashtaroth and Edrei, the the children of Machir, the reward of labour, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that half Gilead and Og's cities were given to Machir's line — God rewarding the labour and valour of his people with possession, the inheritance answering their service, eating the labour of their hands (Ps. 128:2).
32:
"These are the countries which Moses did distribute for inheritance in the plains of Moab, on the other side Jordan, by Jericho, eastward." The witnesses note the summary — these are the lands Moses distributed east of Jordan in the plains of Moab. The witnesses note that Gill notes it is particularly described, that each might know their proper portion. The witnesses note that Clarke notes Moses had settled everything relating to these eastern tribes. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the these are the countries — distributed; the in the plains of Moab — east of Jordan; the that each might know — his portion; the the heritage sure — a name and a place given (Isa. 56:5).

The witnesses agree the verse sums up the eastern settlement, all distributed by Moses "in the plains of Moab": the careful record was made, Gill notes, "that each might know their proper portion"; the orderly distribution, set down for a lasting record, shows God's concern that his people know and hold their appointed inheritance; it teaches that the LORD makes plain to his own what he has given them, that they may possess it with assurance, the heritage recorded and sure, "I will give thee... a name better than of sons and of daughters" (cf. Isa. 56:5) — the eastern lands distributed and recorded by Moses.

So the witnesses make the verse distributed by Moses: The witnesses note the these are the countries: distributed. The witnesses note the in the plains of Moab: east of Jordan. The witnesses note the that each might know: his portion. The witnesses note the the heritage sure: a name and a place given (Isa. 56:5). The expositors observe the these are the countries: distributed. The expositors observe the in the plains of Moab: east of Jordan. The expositors observe the that each might know: his portion. The expositors observe the the heritage sure: a name and a place given (Isa. 56:5). The believer is taught to mark the these are the countries, the in the plains of Moab, the that each might know, the the heritage sure, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that Moses distributed these eastern lands in the plains of Moab — the record made that each might know his portion, God making plain to his people what he has given, the heritage recorded and sure (Isa. 56:5).
33:
"But unto the tribe of Levi Moses gave not any inheritance: the LORD God of Israel was their inheritance, as he said unto them." The witnesses note the climactic word — to Levi no land, for the LORD God of Israel was their inheritance. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses gave Levi no inheritance on either side Jordan, but expressly declared they should have none, though they were his own tribe — showing him disinterested. The witnesses note that the LORD God of Israel was their inheritance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Levi no inheritance — no land; the the LORD their inheritance — god himself; the Moses disinterested — seeking no portion; the the best heritage — the LORD is my portion (Lam. 3:24).

The witnesses agree the chapter closes, as it had paused before, on the highest portion of all — "the LORD God of Israel was their inheritance": Levi received no land, but had God himself for their heritage, a possession above all the tribes' fields; Gill marks that Moses, of this very tribe, sought no portion for his own — a token of his disinterestedness; the repeating of this truth crowns the whole division, that the best inheritance is the LORD himself; it teaches that to have God for one's portion surpasses all earthly possessions, the believer's true and lasting heritage, "the LORD is my portion, saith my soul" (Lam. 3:24), Levi's inheritance the living God.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD their inheritance: The witnesses note the Levi no inheritance: no land. The witnesses note the the LORD their inheritance: God himself. The witnesses note the Moses disinterested: seeking no portion. The witnesses note the the best heritage: the LORD is my portion (Lam. 3:24). The expositors observe the Levi no inheritance: no land. The expositors observe the the LORD their inheritance: God himself. The expositors observe the Moses disinterested: seeking no portion. The expositors observe the the best heritage: the LORD is my portion (Lam. 3:24). The believer is taught to mark the Levi no inheritance, the the LORD their inheritance, the Moses disinterested, the the best heritage, and to seek his inheritance in the LORD. The verse thus records that to Levi was given no land, the LORD God of Israel being their inheritance — the highest portion of all, to have God himself surpassing all earthly possession, the LORD my portion, saith my soul (Lam. 3:24).
